summary: Armin Ronacher argues that porting to Python 3 is manageable but demands deliberate API decisions, especially around text vs bytes. The core challenge is choosing how your library should treat strings now that Python 3’s `str` and `bytes` have incompatible semantics. He outlines practical tactics for byte-based and text-based libraries, including custom 2to3 fixers to handle tricky `__unicode__`/`__str__` patterns and guidance on file I/O to keep behavior consistent across versions. He emphasizes that distribute’s 2to3 support makes dual-version packaging workable without maintaining separate codebases. The biggest migration pain, he says, is testing—especially doctests—so moving to unittest/py.test is advised. His conclusion is pragmatic and urging: even if Python 3 adoption hasn’t gone as hoped, library maintainers should keep porting.
key_points: Decide and document your library’s string/bytes contract before porting; Python 3 makes mixed behavior harder to sustain.
Byte-based libraries need special handling because 2to3 can misconvert byte literals; runtime encoding hacks can preserve bytes.
Text-based libraries often port smoothly, but `__unicode__`/`__str__` patterns require custom fixers to avoid recursion and bytes returns.
Use distribute’s 2to3 integration and custom fixers to build Python 3 outputs without maintaining two source trees.
The hardest part is the test suite; avoid doctests and use unittest/py.test with 2to3-enabled setup testing.
money_quotes: It was surprisingly painless to port the application over but it did require a substantial amount of tweaks and code changes in order to get it running.

Because byte-strings no longer exist in Python 3 and were replaced by a `bytes` object that works similar, but has an incompatible API it is very unlikely that your code will be able to support both in the future.
The issue is that the way you operate on bytes changed fundamentally from Python 2.x to 3.x and 2to3 is not really able to pick it up.
If you call `str()` on your instance now, it will die with a runtime error because it recurses infinitely.
So in Python 3 there is a completely new input/output system. It is very Java-ish and is able to deal with unicode.
Now the biggest problem I had with switching to 3.x: The unittests.
First of all: **do not use doctest**.
Even if Python 3 did not work out as some of us hoped, it is very important that we continue to port libraries over to 3.x.
key_insight: Successful Python 3 ports hinge less on automated conversion and more on explicit string/bytes design choices, disciplined I/O handling, and a testing strategy that survives 2to3’s blind spots.
